yaml.title: 1.1 Perspectives
yaml.description: How our values shape the way we see and treat the environment.

markdown.markdown:
::: {.callout-note title="What this topic covers"} - An **environmental value system (EVS)** is a worldview that shapes how a person or society perceives and acts towards the environment. - An EVS can be modelled as a **system**: *inputs* (education, culture, religion, media, personal experience) lead to *outputs* (decisions, behaviours, policies). - Perspectives sit on a spectrum from **ecocentric** → **anthropocentric** → **technocentric**. ::: A **perspective** is the lens through which someone interprets environmental issues. Two people can look at the same forest and see completely different things — a sacred ecosystem, a carbon store, a source of timber, or a site for development. Those differences come from their **environmental value system**. An EVS is useful because it behaves like a system. Information and experiences go **in**; values, decisions, and actions come **out**. Because the inputs differ from person to person and culture to culture, the outputs — how we treat the environment — differ too. ### The spectrum of perspectives ```{mermaid} flowchart LR A["Ecocentric
nature-centred
minimal intervention"] B["Anthropocentric
people-centred
manage & regulate"] C["Technocentric
technology-centred
solve with innovation"] A --> B --> C style A fill:#cfe9d8,stroke:#2e7d52 style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#caa436 style C fill:#d6e4f0,stroke:#3a6ea5 ``` - **Ecocentric** — puts nature at the centre; values ecological balance and prefers working *with* natural systems over controlling them. - **Anthropocentric** — puts people at the centre; sees the environment as something to be managed sustainably for human benefit, often through regulation and taxes. - **Technocentric** — trusts science and technology to solve environmental problems and to keep raising living standards. Most real people and societies sit *somewhere along* this spectrum rather than at one extreme, and a person's position can shift depending on the issue. ### An EVS as a system ```{mermaid} flowchart LR subgraph Inputs I1[Education] I2[Culture & religion] I3[Media] I4[Personal experience] end EVS([Environmental
Value System]) subgraph Outputs O1[Decisions] O2[Behaviours] O3[Policies] end I1 --> EVS I2 --> EVS I3 --> EVS I4 --> EVS EVS --> O1 EVS --> O2 EVS --> O3 ``` ## Key terms | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | Environmental value system (EVS) | A worldview shaping how an individual or group perceives and acts on the environment. | | Ecocentric | A nature-centred perspective favouring minimal human interference. | | Anthropocentric | A people-centred perspective focused on managing the environment for human use. | | Technocentric | A perspective that relies on technology and innovation to solve environmental problems. | | Perspective | The particular way a person or group interprets an environmental issue. | ## Check yourself ::: {.callout-tip title="Quick questions" collapse="true"} 1. Define *environmental value system* in your own words. 2. Place each on the spectrum: a deep-ecology activist, a government economist, a geo-engineering start-up founder. 3. Explain how **two different inputs** could lead two people to opposite views on building a dam. 4. *To what extent* does a person's EVS stay fixed over their lifetime? ::: ::: {.callout-warning title="Draft"} This is a starter page. Always check wording and emphasis against the official ESS subject guide before relying on it for assessment. :::
